Lab Guide › Communication & Culture

Communicating in the lab

How to reach Christina and each other, plus giving and receiving feedback and resolving conflict.

  • Start guide: Graduate students
  • Start guide: Postdocs

Communicating with Christina

Expectations

  • During the work day, please respond to emails in a timely manner. Christina answers email quickly and appreciates the same in return — but recognizes others work differently, so prioritize replying when you can.
  • Avoid non-urgent emails or Slack messages outside the work day or on weekends unless agreed in advance. Christina does the same (she often schedules messages to send later).
  • Share your specific communication needs.
  • CC Christina when reaching out to people outside the lab for the first time.
  • Grad students: send your course schedule to Christina before registering.

In an emergency, call Christina directly. If she doesn’t pick up, send a text with the details. (Members have her number; contact info is in the member portal.)

Picking the right channel

  • Quick reply: Slack, for urgent or short items.
  • Longer reply: email. If you’re requesting several things, use a numbered list; flag deadlines with date and time. Christina acknowledges email within 1–2 days with an action plan — follow-ups are always welcome.
  • A meeting: stop by the office, or send a when2meet with your requested times and whether it’s a 30- or 60-minute meeting.
  • Documents: please share over email.

Communicating with other lab members

Ask others which methods they prefer, and use topic-specific Slack channels for discussions.

  • All official lab communication should stay professional.
  • Members are expected to resolve conflict among themselves, looping in Christina if further mediation is needed.
  • Undergraduates are always welcome to bring mentorship issues to Christina.

Giving and receiving feedback

Feedback — especially constructive feedback — can be uncomfortable, but it’s a vital part of research.

Giving feedback

  • Make sure the recipient is ready for it; if unsure, ask.
  • Balance warm and cool feedback — name strengths as well as opportunities.
  • Focus on the work, not the person. Be specific; avoid vague “nice job!”.
  • Be timely, but take time to plan primarily-cool feedback.
  • Follow up — feedback is an ongoing process, and note positive changes you see.

Receiving feedback

  • Listen attentively and make sure you understand it, even if you disagree.
  • Ask clarifying questions.
  • Reflect — you’re not obligated to follow all feedback (unless Christina says so); consider a second opinion if you disagree.
  • Follow up to get multiple rounds where possible.

Further reading: 3 simple frameworks to give effective feedback · Receiving and giving effective feedback (Waterloo) · How to give feedback (MIT)

Resolving conflict

Keep Christina in the loop if a problem feels overwhelming or unresolved. Tips for constructive resolution:

  • Raise issues early — silence can lead to escalation or resentment.
  • Define the conflict — is it structural, a communication breakdown, a clash of expectations?
  • Manage your emotions first — collect your thoughts before confronting an issue.
  • Acknowledge your part in creating the conflict.
  • Listen actively — paraphrase, give everyone space to be heard.
  • Ask for help — a neutral third party or mediator can keep things flowing.

“I” statements. A useful frame: “I feel ___ when (you) ___ because ___. What I’m hoping we might try is ___.” Identify your feelings, their cause, the context for why, and the need or ideal you’d like to work toward — adapted to your own voice and culture.

Further reading: Resolving conflict constructively (OSU) · MindTools: conflict resolution · Tips & tools for constructive conflict resolution (Michigan OSCR)
